figures.caption: Figure 5. Visual comparison of TP-Seg with other unified models, including Spider [53], SAM2-UNet [45] ,SegGPT [42] and SR-ICL [8], across the 8 medical lesion segmentation tasks.

Table 2. Quantitative comparison with state-of-the-art methods in terms of Dice and mIoU (%). ↑ indicates that larger scores are better. For specialized models, each task is trained independently, while unified models are trained jointly across the 8 MLS tasks. “Average” denotes the mean performance across all 8 tasks. The top two results are highlighted in green and red.
Figure 6. Visual comparison of component-wise predictions.
Table 3. Ablation studies on the 8 MLS tasks.

Figure 2. Overall architecture of the proposed TP-Seg framework for unified medical lesion segmentation. Each input image, together with its task embedding, is processed by the task-conditioned routing block (TCRB) for feature extraction, followed by the prototype-guided task decoder (PGTD) for task-aware decoding and final lesion prediction.
Figure 4. Illustration of the prototype-guided task decoder.
